faq.title: Frequently asked questions
faq.subtitle: Find answers to the most commonly asked questions

items.question: Is your information about plants reliable?
items.answer: All our plant entries are written using recognised scientific sources: the European Pharmacopoeia, published clinical studies and EFSA recommendations. Every piece of content is reviewed by our team of qualified herbalists.

items.question: How are the plants featured selected?
items.answer: We select plants based on their inclusion in the French and European Pharmacopoeia, their documented traditional use, and the existence of scientific studies confirming their properties. We favour plants that are accessible and commonly used in phytotherapy.

items.question: How do I use the plant encyclopedia?
items.answer: Browse our plant entries by name or by benefit. Each entry details the properties, active compounds, traditional uses, precautions and contraindications. Use the search bar or filters to quickly find what you are looking for.

items.question: How do I prepare a herbal tea or infusion?
items.answer: For a classic infusion, heat water to 80–90°C (do not boil), pour over 1 to 2 teaspoons of dried plant per cup, cover and let steep 5 to 10 minutes depending on the plant. Filter and enjoy. Exact times and temperatures are listed on each plant entry.

items.question: Do medicinal plants replace medical treatment?
items.answer: No. The information on our site is for informational and educational purposes only. It does not constitute medical advice and in no way replaces consultation with a healthcare professional. Always consult your doctor or pharmacist before using medicinal plants.

items.question: Can plants be used during pregnancy?
items.answer: Many plants are not recommended during pregnancy and breastfeeding. We systematically indicate contraindications on each plant entry. In all cases, consult your doctor or midwife before any use of medicinal plants during pregnancy.

items.question: Are there interactions between plants and medications?
items.answer: Yes, some plants can interact with medications. For example, St. John's wort can reduce the effectiveness of many drugs. Known contraindications and interactions are listed on each entry. If you are on medical treatment, always seek advice from your doctor or pharmacist.

items.question: Does the site sell products?
items.answer: Currently, our site is dedicated to information and education about medicinal plants. Our online shop is under development and will be available soon. Subscribe to our newsletter to be notified when it opens.

items.question: How can I contact you?
items.answer: You can email us at contact@remedes-mamie.com, call us on 01 45 85 88 00 or use the contact form on our site. Our team will reply as soon as possible.

items.question: Is the content available in English?
items.answer: Our site is primarily in French, but we are working on the gradual translation of our content into English. Some pages are already available in both languages.

footer.description:
The mission of Grandma's Remedies is to revive the benefits of natural traditions through simple, sincere and effective recipes.

We draw on the heritage of ancestral medicines, enlightened by modern science, to offer everyone formulas proven by time. Our standard: ingredients from their lands of origin, processed in France, in an approach that combines quality, authenticity and safety.

Because caring for yourself also means reconnecting with nature — with love, patience and kindness.

disclaimer.full: The information provided on this website does not constitute medical advice. Dietary supplements do not replace a varied and balanced diet or a healthy lifestyle. Do not exceed the recommended dose. Keep out of reach of children. Consult a healthcare professional before use if you are pregnant, breastfeeding or undergoing medical treatment.
disclaimer.short: Dietary supplement. Does not replace a varied and balanced diet. Do not exceed the recommended dose.
disclaimer.minimal: This website offers dietary supplements, not medicines.
